title: Notes from the Harbor
body:
“I am a sick man. I am a spiteful man. I am an unattractive man. I believe my liver is diseased. However, I know nothing at all about my disease, and do not know for certain what ails me. I don’t consult a doctor for it, and never have, though I have a respect for medicine and doctors. Besides, I am extremely superstitious, sufficiently so to respect medicine, anyway (I am well-educated enough not to be superstitious, but I am superstitious). No, I refuse to consult a doctor from spite. That you probably will not understand. Well, I understand it, though.”

— Fyodor Dostoevsky, Notes from the Underground

I have been going on like this for a long time. Twenty years. More. I am writing these notes at three o’clock in the morning in a laboratory at Stanford University. Somewhere in the corner a centrifuge ticks like a clock that has forgotten how to tell time. Every machine in the lab was accurate, but not humans.

I have not slept. Whenever I close my eyes I see my grandmother’s hand trembling as it reaches for a spoon she can no longer lift. I see my grandfather’s eyes looking past me at something I could not see and will never see. These are my sicknesses. There is no doctor for them. But I was lying when I said I knew nothing about my disease. I know exactly what ails me. I am a man who cannot stop asking who he is. The question has lived in my bones since I was old enough to form memories, and I still wake in the night with this question burning in my throat. It is a question that, like a parasite, has burrowed into the marrow of my bones and made its home. And yet I cannot stop asking, as the moment I stop asking is the moment I become certain, and certainty is the death of the soul. Or so I tell myself.

I fell in love with science because of my grandparents. That is where it began. Or that is where I choose to say it began, which is not the same thing. A beginning is just a place you pick because you cannot bear the thought that there was no beginning, that you have always been this way. I spent my earliest childhood in their care while my parents worked. My mother and father were not absent in any emotional sense, but they were doing what parents do. They were sacrificing presence for provision, hoping the latter might make room for the former. I understood this only much later. As a child I simply knew that my grandparents’ house was where I belonged, as it was the only place where the world made sense.

My grandfather was a smart teacher. He in fact became a principal of one of the best high schools in town, and was recognized by the Minister of Education. He never mentioned this. I learned it from others. He was a man of such extraordinary intellect that I, as a child, believed he knew everything. The reason the sky was blue, why electrical cords grew hot, why airplanes left white scars across the sky… he had answers for all of it, and he gave them to me with a patience that I only now understand as love. But I did not want his love – I wanted his answers. I wanted them because I believed, I genuinely believed, that if I could only collect enough answers, the questions would stop. I was lying. The questions never stopped. They multiplied. I was ten years old and reading undergraduate textbooks. I was eleven and copying equations I did not understand into notebooks. I was twelve and asked why the teachers demanded three exact folders of colors x y and z, why pens instead of pencils, why compliance was valued above reasoning. I have always been like this. I cannot accept an arbitrary rule, and look at a convention and simply obey it. I guess this is a disease of some kind, but I say this without the slightest embarrassment, though I am embarrassed by everything else.

My grandmother was a teacher for forty-five years. She stood in classrooms and gave children language. She made flashcards by hand when her eyes were tired. She made yogurt and fed it to me when I was five and all the while of course, I asked too many questions. She was my harbor. She was the only place where the water was calm. But some part of me knew that safety was temporary, that the world would demand I leave, and that I would have to go. So I asked my questions faster. I collected my answers more urgently. I was building a raft out of knowledge, and I did not notice that the water was already rising.

My paternal grandfather had Alzheimer’s disease. I was five. What does a five-year-old understand about Alzheimer’s? He understands that a man who used to know his name no longer knows it. He understands that a hand which used to be strong now cannot hold a spoon. I fed my grandfather. I lifted the spoon to his mouth because his hand would not obey him. I had already learned that the self can dissolve like sugar in water. One day when I came back from visiting him, my maternal grandfather asked me at eye level whether I had had a good time. He always spoke to children at eye level, as though we were real people. I told him the truth. I said it was not fun. I said the old man had no reaction. I said he was boring. I watched my mother’s face darken and I did not understand why. I asked why. Why should I not say what I felt. Why was honesty a crime.

She told me I had spoken the words she least wanted to hear. I see now that she was not angry. She was grieving, and she wanted me to be kind to a man who was disappearing, and I did not know how to be kind to a disappearing man. I only knew how to be honest, and honesty in the face of dementia is a violence. My grandfather passed away not long after.

I cried. I cried because everyone else was crying and I did not know why. I was selfish. All children are selfish. Or perhaps selfishness is the natural condition of a creature who has not yet learned that other people have inner lives as real and urgent as his own. Perhaps growing up is nothing more than the slow, brutal education in the reality of other people’s pain.

The last day I spent with my maternal grandparents before returning to my mother in Seoul, I hid in a storage room. She called my name and I pressed myself against the wall behind sacks of rice and did not answer. She found me eventually. She laughed about it later, or tried to. But there was a bitterness in the laughter, because she knew what I was doing even if I did not. I was trying to make myself small enough to live inside love forever. I was trying to prove that if I refused to leave, love could not make me go.

Nevermind. I was not a philosopher. I was a frightened child, and there is no philosophy in fear. But I am still that frightened child. I have been hiding ever since. I hid when they bullied me in Seoul. I hide now in this laboratory, surrounded by equipment and books and the smell of disinfectant, the pungent smell of isopropanol… pretending that if I can just understand enough, the world will become safe again. But the world is not safe, the world has never been safe. And understanding does not make it so. I know this. I have always known this. And knowing it has never stopped me from trying to understand. In Seoul I learned what cruelty was. Not the cruelty of adults, which has reasons, however twisted. The cruelty of children, which has no reason. I was bullied for being ugly and short and dumb. I stood my ground because I knew my grades were not actually bad, but standing your ground does not prevent the ground from dissolving beneath your feet. I learned their language and social cues by watching the popular boys from the corner where I sat. I tried to imitate them and I only looked like a fool. They had learned to joke and banter on playgrounds while I was learning the periodic table from my grandfather. They had learned to navigate the social world while I was learning to conjugate English verbs with my grandmother. I do not regret what I learned instead, however. I only regret that no one told me there would be a test in the other subject, and that I would fail it so completely. For it is not knowledge that saves us. It is the ability to move through the world without being destroyed by it. And I have never had that ability.

I have always had too much consciousness. To be too conscious is an illness. For man’s everyday needs, it would have been quite enough to have the ordinary human consciousness, half or a quarter of what I have. But I have always noticed too much. I noticed everything. I notice the way a person’s eyes move when they are lying. I notice the tremor in my grandmother’s hand before she notices it herself. I notice that the question of who I am has no answer, and I cannot stop noticing it. The boys who bullied me were happy. I am sure of it. They were happy because they were stupid, and stupidity is a gift. The stupid man does not ask why. He accepts, and he conforms. He becomes what the world wants him to become, and in becoming it, he is at peace. But I could not conform. I asked why. And for this, I was punished.

Not officially though. Officially, I was a good student. In eighth grade I entered a government-sponsored program for gifted students at Sejong Science High School. It was a gift. It gave me knowledge but demanded I prove myself worthy every day. I did not mind. I was used to proving myself. But what I did not understand, what I am only beginning to understand now, is that knowledge without love is a starvation. You can fill your mind with every fact in the universe and still be empty. You can solve every equation and still not know who you are.

Then I moved to America in the middle of eighth grade. I stopped everything midyear because I could not bear the arbitrariness anymore. I wanted to be free from the mimetic competition, the mindless knowledge, the bureaucracies that demanded irrational things simply because they said so. Moving felt like liberation, because people left me alone. In Korea, my strangeness was a wound, and wounds for what they are maggots shall always probe them. In America, I made it to Bronx High School of Science, and then Stanford. I kept asking where my life was headed. The horizon kept receding. Each achievement revealed a larger ocean.

But the whole point of sailing was not to arrive but to become someone who knows how to navigate. This sounds like wisdom, but it is not wisdom. I am tired of sailing. I am tired of being my own worst critic. I am tired of the question that eats me alive.

But I am lying again. I am not tired. If I were tired I would stop, take a rest. My family tells me to rest. They say I spend too much time in lecture halls and labs. They do not understand that I cannot rest. Who gets it anyways? If I rest, I let my own identity down. I am who I am because of the people who shaped me, and they have succumbed to disease. Parkinson’s stealing my grandmother. The Alzheimer’s that took my grandfather before I knew how to love him. The tumor that grew in my mother’s body while she cared for mine. And so I work. I work because working is the only way I know to love them back.

My grandmother was diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease. That was fourteen years ago. Fourteen years of watching strength become helplessness, piece by piece, like a beautiful cathedral being dismantled stone by stone. First the tremors in her hand. Then her legs. Then the wheelchair, then the memory, and so on. Once at the dinner table, I showed her a photograph of my mother and she said she did not know who the woman was. I was a little shocked. I told her the woman’s name was Yangsoo, Yangsoo is her daughter. And I looked down at my rice bowl so no one would see my eyes. This is what families do. We hide tears in rice bowls. We believe that if we do not name the pain, the pain will be smaller.

The same woman who taught me everything, who made flashcards by hand so I could learn to read, who gave forty-five years to teaching children language, could not lift her own spoon. She always loved taking care of flowers. She has a whole garden of flowers she waters everyday. She had always said canes were for weak old people and she would be different. She had always been the strong one. And now she looked defeated and sad, and I hated, I hated with such hatred, what disease takes from people. She told me she wished she could make the same yogurt she used to make when I was five. I told her I could go buy yogurt by myself now, from a supermarket. She smiled as if I had misunderstood everything.

Obviously she was not talking about yogurt. She was talking about the self that was disappearing, the self that had strength and purpose. That self was leaving us, and the yogurt was only a symbol of everything else already gone.

No, I’m wrong. She was probably talking about yogurt. She wanted to make yogurt for her grandson because making yogurt was something she could do, and now she could do nothing. I am the one who turns everything into philosophy. I’m wrong, as meaning does not help. Meaning does not make anyone happier. Meaning does not stop tremors. Meaning is what you reach for when you have reached the end of action, and I have reached the end of action. I massage her swollen legs because of spontaneous spasms that the disease gave, along with poor circulation. I watch her frown in pain quietly, and from afar, I cry in the darkness where no one can see. And I write these notes, which help no one.

In 2023, before Stanford, we went to Hokkaido together. She had insisted on seeing her old place one last time, as it was the place she had left as a girl on a ship to Korea, when her family worked there under colonial Japan. “It’s that one harbor I want to see, I still remember my mother…”, she proclaims. She wanted to touch where her story began before the disease took her chance to return. On the first day, after her favorite meal with sushi, she collapsed. Her body convulsed, foaming at her lips. My head went blank and I became detached. I checked her pulse. I positioned her body. I recited what I had learned about seizure management as if I were taking an exam – like a student performing well under pressure. I learned to detach from pain. You become a machine because the alternative is to become a boy watching his grandmother collapse over sushi in a foreign country. When I visit Korea now I am partly a grandson and partly a nurse. I massage her swollen legs. I think about the flashcards she made. The place she left as a girl. The place she built for me. Both fading, and there’s no difference.

And I am further from shore than I have ever been.

I have cried more nights than I have told anyone. No, nevermind, since there’s more tragedies that people go through and I’m too privileged. This is such a me-centered view of the world. I repent in the cathedral of knowledge I tried to build, again, in this laboratory.

My grandmother left her home once as a girl on a ship, not knowing what waited. And still she built a life. Forty-five years of teaching. Flashcards and yogurt and love made from what history handed her.

I left mine too. Nothing but questions, with a stubborn belief that answers waited on the other side of the water. And I am still asking, I am still sailing. The horizon recedes and I keep moving forward. I am not who I was, and I am not yet who I will be. I am the space between the port and the horizon, and that is exactly where I am supposed to be.

Somewhere in the vast and terrifying middle of the journey, there is a man who does not know himself, and who has finally understood that not knowing is the only way to keep becoming.
